critic_raw:
Sensor readings collected every second from 100+ fabrication tools over 24 months represent an enormous amount of data with extreme temporal autocorrelation at the per-second timescale. A stratified random split at what granularity — individual seconds, individual sensor windows, individual tool-run episodes? If the split is at the second level, adjacent time steps from the same tool run appear in both train and test, and the LSTM will have effectively 'seen' the pre-failure context of every failure event during training. If the split is at the tool-run level, the temporal structure within runs is preserved, but inter-run dependencies are ignored.

The F1-score is listed as the primary metric 'balancing false negatives and positives.' In semiconductor manufacturing, the cost asymmetry between a false negative (missing an impending failure, causing unplanned downtime and wafer scrapping) and a false positive (unnecessary preventive maintenance) is typically very large and domain-specific. Using the default F1 (which gives equal weight to precision and recall) ignores this asymmetry. The business goal of 'significantly reducing downtime' requires prioritizing recall, but the metric used for model selection does not enforce this priority.

Production shifts and maintenance schedules are described as confounds that are 'controlled for by stratifying based on these factors.' But stratifying the split by production shift does not prevent the model from learning shift-specific patterns — it only ensures each shift is represented in train and test. To test whether shift-specific confounds are a problem, the design would need to either hold out an entire shift or explicitly test for shift-by-model interaction effects.

The logistic regression baseline uses 'engineered time-series features' but the specific features are not described. Time-series feature engineering quality (e.g., tsfresh vs. simple mean/std) has a large effect on logistic regression performance, and a poorly engineered baseline will make the LSTM look better than it actually is relative to a well-specified traditional model.
all_issues_raised: The granularity of the stratified random split is unspecified — if split at the per-second level, adjacent time steps from the same tool run leak across train and test, giving the LSTM effective pre-failure context for every failure event during training.

Using default F1-score for model selection gives equal weight to precision and recall, but the stated business goal of reducing downtime requires prioritizing recall — the metric used to select the model is misaligned with the operational objective.

Stratifying the split by production shift ensures representation of each shift but does not prevent the model from learning shift-specific patterns, so it does not actually control for shift as a confound.

The time-series features engineered for the logistic regression baseline are unspecified, making it impossible to verify whether the baseline is competitive or a straw man weakened by poor feature engineering.
all_issues_adjudicated: The granularity of the stratified random split is unspecified — if split at the per-second level, adjacent time steps from the same tool run leak across train and test, giving the LSTM effective pre-failure context for every failure event during training.

Using default F1-score for model selection gives equal weight to precision and recall, but the stated business goal of reducing downtime requires prioritizing recall — the metric used to select the model is misaligned with the operational objective.

Stratifying the split by production shift ensures representation of each shift but does not prevent the model from learning shift-specific patterns, so it does not actually control for shift as a confound.

The time-series features engineered for the logistic regression baseline are unspecified, making it impossible to verify whether the baseline is competitive or a straw man weakened by poor feature engineering.
